summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier speculation about whether the DMA might push tech giants to leave the EU market. He corrects a transcription error about Apple's EU revenue share (7% of App Store, not absolute revenue) but argues the distinction likely doesn't matter much. He dissects remarks from EU commissioners Vestager and Breton, highlighting what he sees as overreach in demanding Photos be uninstallable and in rejecting Meta's pay-or-consent advertising model. Gruber finds Breton's confident assertion that no gatekeeper can afford to leave the EU market to be debatable, especially for Meta. He argues the EC is effectively doing product design rather than regulation, and that their stance on targeted advertising contradicts what most users actually prefer.
key_points: The 7% figure refers specifically to Apple's EU App Store revenue, not total revenue, but Gruber argues the two are likely similar since App Store spending should track overall revenue by region.
The EC's demand that Photos be fully uninstallable goes beyond regulation into product design territory, since Photos serves as the system-level camera roll with deep OS integration.
Commissioner Breton openly boasts that the EU's 450-million-customer market gives them leverage because it's 'unthinkable' for gatekeepers to leave — Gruber finds this very much thinkable.
The EC rejects Meta's pay-or-consent model despite Vestager herself advocating for exactly such a paid Facebook option in 2018, and despite users overwhelmingly choosing free-with-ads.
If Meta concedes to offering free service without targeted ads in the EU, regulators worldwide could demand the same, potentially devastating Meta's global revenue model.
money_quotes: This is product design, not mere regulation.
Why stop there? Why not mandate that Springboard — the Home Screen — be a replaceable component? Or the entire OS itself? Why are iPhone users required to use iOS?
Privacy fundamentalists can't seem to accept that most people don't share their fervor that consensual targeted advertising is inherently wrong. Most people see it as a good deal.

Why exactly would Meta choose to remain in the EU if they're forced to offer their services for pennies on the dollar (or in this case, cents on the euro)? Out of the goodness of Mark Zuckerberg's heart?
A provoking thought indeed, but apparently this was only worth exploring until they found out that EU citizens would overwhelmingly consent to free services with targeted ads.

Breton lays bare the EC's belief that they hold all the cards — that it is 'unthinkable' for any of the designated gatekeepers not to conduct business in the EU, and that 'none of them can afford not to be in our market.'
They don't want to eliminate all targeted advertising, just Meta's (and Google's), but that's hard to put into written law while claiming not to be targeting specific American companies.
I, for one, consider it very thinkable.
key_insight: The European Commission's DMA enforcement has crossed from market regulation into dictating product design and business models, and its confident assumption that tech giants have no choice but to comply may prove dangerously wrong.
